title: Groupthink: How Smart Teams Make Catastrophic Decisions
description: The Bay of Pigs invasion, the Challenger disaster, the 2008 financial collapse — all involved groups of intelligent, qualified people making catastrophically wrong decisions. The reason is a single psychological mechanism.

segments.voice: On April 17, 1961, 1,400 CIA-trained Cuban exiles landed at the Bay of Pigs with the expectation of igniting a national uprising that would topple Fidel Castro. Within three days, they had been captured or killed. The invasion was a complete strategic failure. It had been planned and approved by some of the most experienced foreign policy minds in Washington — the Joint Chiefs of Staff, the CIA, and the President's closest advisors. How did some of the most intelligent, experienced decision-makers in the world approve such a deeply flawed plan? Irving Janis spent years finding out. The answer was groupthink.
segments.visual: Bay of Pigs Cuba 1961 invasion CIA military failure

segments.voice: Janis, a social psychologist at Yale and UC Berkeley, coined the term groupthink in 1972 after analyzing multiple American foreign policy disasters. He defined it as a mode of thinking that people engage in when they are deeply involved in a cohesive in-group, when members' strivings for unanimity override their motivation to realistically appraise alternative courses of action. The group becomes more concerned with maintaining harmony and consensus than with making accurate decisions. The desire to belong and agree defeats the purpose of having a group at all.
segments.visual: group meeting consensus harmony approval psychology

segments.voice: Janis identified eight symptoms of groupthink. Illusion of invulnerability: the group develops excessive optimism that encourages extreme risks. Collective rationalization: members discount warnings that might lead them to reconsider assumptions. Belief in the group's inherent morality: members believe in the rightness of their cause, ignoring the ethical or moral consequences of their decisions. Stereotyped views of out-groups: out-groups are seen as too weak, evil, or stupid to represent a serious threat. Pressure on dissenters: members who express arguments against the group's stereotypes are pressured to conform. Self-censorship: members avoid deviating from the apparent consensus.
segments.visual: groupthink symptoms eight criteria Janis chart

segments.voice: The remaining two symptoms are particularly insidious. Illusion of unanimity: the false perception that everyone in the group agrees — partly because dissent is not voiced, silence is interpreted as consent. And mindguards: self-appointed members who protect the group from information that might challenge the shared complacency. In the Bay of Pigs planning process, CIA officials who had doubts about the plan suppressed them in meetings. Officials who received intelligence questioning the plan's viability did not bring it to the full advisory group. The mindguards operated effectively.
segments.visual: illusion unanimity silence mindguard information suppression

segments.voice: The Challenger space shuttle disaster of January 28, 1986 is the most technically documented groupthink case in history. The night before the launch, engineers at Morton Thiokol, which manufactured the solid rocket boosters, held an urgent teleconference with NASA officials. They recommended postponing the launch because O-ring seals in the boosters had never been tested at temperatures as low as the predicted launch temperature of 18 degrees Fahrenheit. Their concern was technically valid — the O-rings failed the next morning and Challenger disintegrated 73 seconds after launch, killing seven astronauts.

The teleconference transcript is a documented record of groupthink in action. NASA managers responded to Thiokol's concerns with pressure and frustration. One NASA manager said they had to 'put on their management hat, not their engineering hat.' Under this pressure, Thiokol management overruled their own engineers and approved the launch. The engineers who had raised concerns were excluded from the final approval decision. The Presidential Commission investigating the disaster found that the organizational culture at NASA had suppressed safety concerns through a hierarchy that prioritized schedule over safety. The decision-making process was broken by design.
segments.visual: NASA management hierarchy pressure engineers O-ring

segments.voice: The 2008 financial crisis provides a contemporary groupthink example at institutional scale. Multiple analyses, including the Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission report, documented how investment banks, ratings agencies, and regulatory bodies collectively failed to recognize the systemic risk building in mortgage-backed securities. Not because the information wasn't available — it was. But within each institution, the prevailing consensus was that housing prices wouldn't fall significantly at a national level. Those who raised concerns were marginalized. Those who profited from the consensus had structural incentives to reinforce it. The illusion of unanimity held until the system collapsed.
segments.visual: 2008 financial crisis Wall Street bank collapse mortgage

segments.voice: Social conformity pressure is the engine of groupthink, and its power extends far beyond cohesive expert groups. Solomon Asch's conformity experiments of the 1950s are the foundational demonstration. Asch had groups of participants judge which of three lines on a card matched a reference line — a trivially easy visual task with an obvious answer. But all other 'participants' in the room were confederates, and they had been instructed to give the wrong answer. When alone, participants gave the correct answer 99 percent of the time. When the confederates unanimously gave the wrong answer, participants conformed about 37 percent of the time.
segments.visual: Asch conformity experiment line judgment pressure

segments.voice: What made Asch's findings more disturbing than the compliance rate was the qualitative data. When participants explained why they had given the wrong answer, many genuinely reported that they began to doubt their own perception. They saw what everyone else said they saw. The social pressure didn't just make people capitulate — it altered their perception. The brain revised its sensory interpretation to match the social consensus. In a high-stakes group setting, with experts rather than strangers, with years of shared culture rather than minutes of experimental pressure, this effect is dramatically stronger.
segments.visual: visual perception conformity social pressure doubt reality

segments.voice: Status hierarchies within groups dramatically amplify groupthink. Research by Nemeth and Staw found that high-status group members have disproportionate influence on decisions, and that their positions tend to anchor group consensus. Lower-status members suppress dissent because of the social cost of challenging a superior — both the formal consequences and the informal signaling that you are not a team player. The president's inner circle doesn't disagree with the president not because they lack opinions, but because the social architecture of the group makes disagreement costly.
segments.visual: hierarchy status group leader authority suppress dissent

segments.voice: Time pressure accelerates groupthink. Research on decision-making under time constraints shows that groups under pressure use less information, consider fewer alternatives, and reach closure faster — prioritizing speed over accuracy. Emergency decisions made by cohesive groups under time pressure show the strongest groupthink effects. The military context, the boardroom during a crisis, the operating room — exactly the settings where decision quality is most critical — are precisely the settings where time pressure most severely compromises it.
segments.visual: time pressure crisis decision making rushed deadline

segments.voice: Diversity within groups is the most robustly supported structural antidote to groupthink. Research by Katherine Phillips at Columbia Business School found that groups with one member from a different background — even when that member contributed nothing substantively different — produced more creative and more accurate decisions than homogeneous groups. The mere presence of someone perceived as different increased the group's willingness to voice disagreement and consider alternative hypotheses. Diversity doesn't just add perspectives. It changes the social permission structure for dissent.
segments.visual: diverse team meeting different perspectives innovation

segments.voice: The devil's advocate role was historically used in the Catholic Church's canonization process — a specific individual assigned to argue against sainthood, however overwhelming the case for it. Research by Charlan Nemeth and colleagues found that assigning a devil's advocate significantly improved group decision quality in laboratory studies. However, the benefit was stronger when the devil's advocate genuinely disagreed rather than merely performing the role by assignment. Groups that knew the devil's advocate was playing a role partially discounted their contributions. Real dissent is more valuable than assigned dissent.
segments.visual: devil's advocate role assigned dissent Catholic Church

segments.voice: Pre-mortem analysis, proposed by Gary Klein and popularized by Daniel Kahneman, is one of the most practically validated interventions against groupthink. Before a decision is finalized, the group is asked to imagine that the decision was implemented and failed catastrophically. They are then asked to generate explanations for the failure. This exercise changes the social permission structure: because the frame is hypothetical, individuals can voice concerns without appearing to challenge the group or the leadership. Pre-mortems routinely surface concerns that were suppressed in regular discussions.
segments.visual: pre-mortem analysis failure scenario planning group

segments.voice: Anonymous feedback mechanisms — suggestion boxes, anonymous surveys, blind voting before discussion — reduce the conformity pressure that drives self-censorship. Research on anonymous versus identified voting shows that anonymous conditions produce more honest assessments and more disagreement. This is why many organizations have moved to anonymous pulse surveys and anonymous retrospectives: they create a technical workaround for the social pressure that produces groupthink. The anonymity doesn't eliminate the bias. It reduces the social cost of expressing it.
segments.visual: anonymous feedback survey voting blind mechanism

segments.voice: The concept of psychological safety, developed by Amy Edmondson at Harvard Business School through research in hospital teams and technology companies, has become central to understanding groupthink prevention. Edmondson defined psychological safety as the shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Teams with high psychological safety reported more errors — not because they made more errors, but because members felt safe admitting them. High-performing medical teams weren't the teams that made no mistakes. They were the teams that could talk about mistakes without social penalty.
segments.visual: psychological safety team Amy Edmondson Google research

segments.voice: Google's Project Aristotle, a large-scale internal study of team effectiveness published in 2016, found that psychological safety was the single strongest predictor of team performance among all the variables studied — far more predictive than team composition, individual talent, or resource availability. The teams that performed best were not the teams with the most talented individuals. They were the teams where every member felt comfortable speaking up. Groupthink is ultimately a failure of psychological safety. And psychological safety is a function of leadership behavior.
segments.visual: Google Project Aristotle team performance psychological safety

segments.voice: Leaders who want to prevent groupthink in their own teams face a paradox: the behaviors that make leaders effective in the short term — projecting certainty, maintaining authority, suppressing conflict — are precisely the behaviors that produce groupthink in the long term. Leaders who invite challenge, acknowledge uncertainty, and visibly reward dissent create a different social permission structure. The challenge is that leaders are also subject to groupthink pressures — they too want confirmation and harmony. The antidote has to begin with the person who shapes the group's culture. That is always the leader.
segments.visual: leader humility uncertainty invitation challenge team culture

segments.voice: Janis's groupthink model has been critiqued for being better as a post-hoc explanatory framework than a predictive tool. Not all cohesive groups make bad decisions, and not all bad decisions result from groupthink. But the core observation — that group cohesion and conformity pressure systematically compromise decision quality — has been supported by decades of subsequent experimental research. The disasters it explains are not anomalies. They are the predictable outcome of putting smart people in rooms, giving them authority, depriving them of dissenting voices, and calling it a decision-making process.
segments.visual: smart people meeting authority no dissent decision failure
